item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of dance as a vector for memetic infection, but the symptoms aren't really that interesting. Also, it's amnestics, not amnesiac drugs.

Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I knew someone would have written a scip based on the dancing plage of 1518! I just knew it! Having said that, I would have really preferred you to stick closer to the original event. Dancing until you die, being completely aware of what you are doing but being unable to stop is a charming idea, this… reads like a joke that's not funny and not even dangerous (It does stuff to you, but it doesn't kill you). I can't upvote this knowing how great it could have been.

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In other words, "Party Rock Anthem" is this idea done as a -J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have mixed feelings over this article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't find it original enough to be interesting. ''Uncontrollable infectious dancing or singing'' is a pretty common theme. That being said, it's still well done, so a total of +0. Also, to me it almost feels like a joke SCP; taking something that exists in real life (the catchy-ness of a new dance) and describing it as anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't know how to say it, but this article just isn't clicking with me. I don't necessarily hate it, it's decent in all aspects, but I just don't like it.
I'll read this again later, and maybe then I'll stop being neutral.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This article is up for review. Check it out, it needs more attention.

I like this one. That being said, I think it could be better. I like that there's only a hint of weird in the actions caused by SCP-1247 transition things, but I feel like the method of transmission is a bit unclear. More detail on which movements are the "key series" would be cool, and in general I'd like to see more detail on what the dance itself looks like, since recordings are safe. But, even with that complaint, I found this to be clever and well-written enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for contagious dancing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They deserved it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of the Dancing Plague of 1518.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now how do you expect this SCP to be contained when this documentery is out for 7 years as of this post?
http://www.imdb.com/title/tt0436724/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: lol Naw. This actually reminds me of Safety Dance. We can dance! We can dance! Everybody look at your hands!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A date rape dance. Sounds about right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this may have done it. Still a little iffy on the orange color thing, but the fact that they can't stop dancing is now the far more salient effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damnit. Now I'm humming the bunny hop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And apparently my spellcheck wasn't working when I did the rewrite. :-/

Thanks, Voct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've taken a bunch of the feedback from this thread and done a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's just ridiculous, as is the idea that the Foundation has contacts who hang around at parties in case something goes amiss.

Mobile Task Force 1999 ("Wallflower Child") finally gets its moment to shine!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Going by the partial Caribbean origins, what about a watery theming?
Affected subjects act as though they were a model of a pool of water of equal volume. Including the bit about not going uphill. Imagine the dancers coming out of a party up a steep hill. With heavy traffic.
If there is an object in the vicinity of affected subjects, they will pick it up and ferry it as part of the dance the same way that the currents in aforementioned pool would drag that object around (or tug at it, if the current wouldn't be strong enough). Objects include people or other living things not dancing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like several things here.
-Dance as a means of communication, and hence a channel for memetics.
-A meme scp that propagates passively instead of forcing the subject to spread it "This feels so good! You should totally try it!" versus "You most look at The Pattern. The Pattern demands it!"
-A memetic without an implied shadow-agenda creator. I get a feeling that this just kinda happened, and then spread more or less naturally; not that is was designed by unknowable extra-dimensional bugaboos to drive men mad in preparation for their arrival.

But there is just no reason for this to be an SCP. How would anybody tell the difference between this and, say, the macarena? There has to be some extra oomph before any agent would pay attention, much less bring it in to base for containment. Maybe the dance could be addictive, with subjects gradually doing nothing but dancing till they physically can't, eventually dieing of dehydration. I'm thinking of a meth-like effect, with maybe a slowed-down onset before obvious negative side-effects kick in.

Given that it was tossed off in an hour and a half, not bad. But it could be a lot better with a little more investment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of a proprioceptive meme is not a bad one, it's just that as far as I can tell none of its effects here would be out of place among people who dance in clubs at night. Maybe if afterwards their muscle memory gets wiped or they end up stuck on their hands and knees with reduced capability for speech on top of what you already have. Because not only does having a bunch of partiers that end up bleating like helpless donkeys make an obvious Pinocchio reference, it's kinda scary in my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the idea itself could work as a -J, just not this article. If you played up the absurdity and made the 'scared of ravers in orange pants' thing more explicit, you could probably make a decent joke out the researchers involved being paranoid and overly conservative. The way it is presented here isn't funny as it stands now, so it'd make a terrible -J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's more like some dimwit at the Foundation is scared of ravers in orange pants or something.

This is exactly what I was thinking. I was expecting this to be a -J or an -EX. "Them youngin's are doing the devil's dancing again! It makes 'em loose and liberal and orange!"

That said, a memetic dance craze is a great idea, I just don't think this has the execution down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, -J's are supposed to be funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this seems like it would make more sense as a -j.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted since I have a deep dislike of articles that aren't clearly anomalous in nature. This seems like the sort of thing which even the Foundation couldn't prove to be worthy of SCP qualification.

The 'discovery' paragraph also strikes me as off. Why and how would the observer have been able to notice the anomalous effect? It doesn't present itself if you watch it, so they couldn't have realised they were being affected. And honestly, who the hell is going to see that some ravers started wearing the colour orange and became a bit more suggestible and then immediately link it to the dance and call the Foundation? That's just ridiculous, as is the idea that the Foundation has contacts who hang around at parties in case something goes amiss.

The main problem I have with this article, I think, is that you really didn't go into enough detail. I imagine that you were trying to keep the article short, which is prefectly fine, but you really haven't examined the symptoms on a level where they actually jump off the page and make it obvious this is an SCP. Saying that they are more suggestible in a single line and not mentioning it again doesn't make it sound disturbing, really unusual, or frankly even all that interesting. If you examined the details a little more closely, and maybe added some more obviously unusual symptoms, it would work a lot better.

Also, this is probably just me, but the line "News reports should be investigated under the pretense of an outbreak of a dangerous strain of influenza" doesn't make the slightest bit of sense to me. Mind explaining what you meant?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's more like some dimwit at the Foundation is scared of ravers in orange pants or something.

This is 100% not what I was thinking when I wrote this and is now my preferred interpretation.

This one grew out of a discussion on chat about memetics, when someone mentioned the idea of a proprioceptive meme. I was hoping that the increased suggestibility would make readers wonder who made this thing up, and why. Should I make it more obviously disturbing, or is this just not a good concept in your opinion?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) I don't think this counts as a memetic agent, since merely seeing the dance or knowing about it has no effect. Gaining knowledge of how to do an SCP merely by observing it being done doesn't make it memetic.

It seems that actually preforming the dance yourself is the key trigger, and that's fine. Communication of information can come in forms other than mere observation. Once you experience it you are infected, and it is very much a shared experience that infected people encourage you to participate in.

I think the larger problem with this SCP is that it is not very good, and hardly even strikes me as an SCP. It's more like some dimwit at the Foundation is scared of ravers in orange pants or something. They are doing the devil dance and it makes them have the good times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) I don't think this counts as a memetic agent, since merely seeing the dance or knowing about it has no effect. Gaining knowledge of how to do an SCP merely by observing it being done doesn't make it memetic.

2) Using DMCA take down requests wouldn't work very well, since:

2a) It only works if the server hosting the video is in the United States.

2b) A take down request can be challenged on the grounds that the issuer of the request doesn't actually own the copyrights. While in some circumstances the Foundation might be able to manufacture evidence that they do own the copyrights to the video, I don't think they'd be able to do so in all circumstances. And no one can have a copyright or trademark for an improvisational style of dance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't sell me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is my attempt at Bright's first challenge: SCP in 24 hours, no sandbox, no feedback.

It took me about 90 minutes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is silly in an awesome way. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for liking orange.
